By Anna Gaca Rap RX Rico Nasty Working once again with Kenneth Blume, the high-energy rapper revisits her signature sound for a tight 20-minute album. It’s her most focused and satisfying work in years. By Mehan Jayasuriya Rock You’re Gonna Need a Little Music Yard Act The UK band smooths out its characteristically jagged post-punk for a vaguely uplifting album that brings to mind lots of older British rock groups. By Victoria Borlando Electronic The Friendliest Fight We Know Oen Sujet The singular Montreal collective, led by composer John Winston Phillips, returns after 17 years, committed as ever to its unique brand of labyrinthine, cathartic art-pop. By Maxie Younger Rap Set in Stone Rick Ross Some flashes of inspiration, some familiar retreads, some egregious missteps, lots of money talk—it’s another Rick Ross album. By Dash Lewis See More More Reviews Experimental Tranquilizer Oneohtrix Point Never Best New Album Drawing on a cache of commercial sample CDs, Daniel Lopatin assembles an impossibly dense and transportive electronic album that takes impermanence as its inspiration. Pop/R&B Addison Addison Rae The girlypop album of summer warrants comparison to Lana, Madonna, and most of all, Britney. Are we ready for Addison? Rock Snocaps Snocaps The Crutchfield twins—accompanied by MJ Lenderman and Brad Cook—reunite for a spirited new project that plays to their strengths and embraces all the miles they’ve traveled. Pop/R&B LUX Rosalía Best New Album The Spanish singer’s fourth record is a heartfelt offering of avant-garde classical pop that roars through genre, romance, and religion. Rap Stardust Danny Brown Working with a new vanguard of outsider electronic artists, the Detroit rapper lets his freak flag fry. The results are shaky but full of heart. Pop/R&B West End Girl Lily Allen With an album that doubles as an insider’s account of a tabloid divorce, the singer finds a new evolution of her signature style: Lightness isn’t a foil for irony, but a vehicle for hurt. Rap Lil Herb G Herbo Stepping into his teenage shoes for a loose concept album, the Chicago icon reflects on the original era of drill with the clarity of a scene veteran. Electronic Repulsor Shlohmo The L.A. beatmaker turns aggressive on his fourth album—dialing up the distortion, flooding his beats with overdriven synths, and pushing anxious moods into the red. I missed all of this buzz-worthy carrying on and came to their latest release completely tabula rasa. Sometimes that’s the best way to greet a band with established history, as you bring no expectations or biases developed from past experience. The version of Ripper I encountered on their 3rd album Towards Rebirth are coming off a nearly 10-year layoff to deliver mega-aggressive, speed-drenched death-thrashery of an impressive caliber.” Ripping the birth canal. Sallow Moth – Hydrophilous Brood Review By Grin Reaper on July 23, 2026 in Reviews , Death Metal , 26 comments “Less than a year after delivering one of my favorite albums of 2025, Sallow Moth flutters in to unload another monstrous helping of progressive death metal. Thematically, Hydrophilous Brood continues exploring the quantum entanglements of the Mossbane Lantern , a capriciously powerful artifact that can transport users across planes of existenceâoften with horrific consequences. I thoroughly enjoyed this backdrop on Mossbane Lantern , and learning that Sallow Moth ’s new platter would once again fathom the inscrutable fickleness of the Lantern made me equal parts giddy and nervous.” Moth tossing. Into Darkness – Route to the Outer Side Review By Creeping Ivy on July 23, 2026 in Reviews , Death Doom , Death Metal , 20 comments “As AMG readers surely know, death metal and science fiction go together like science and fiction. Foundational and contemporary examples alike confirm that atonal riffing, guttural growling, and machine-gun drumming pair well with spaceships, aliens, and exoplanets. On the surface, Italy’s Into Darkness fit nicely into this milieuâthat cover recalls the content of Spheres ( Pestilence ) and the color palette of The Key ( Nocturnus ). The band insists, however, that they do not play ‘sci-fi’ or ‘spacey’ death metal. Instead, Into Darkness describe Route to the Outer Side , their long-gestating debut album, as timeless death metal with a focus on ‘divulgative astronomy,’ which translates complex science into accessible language.” Nerds B raging. Imperium – Exodus Unknown Review By Saunders on July 22, 2026 in Reviews , Groove Metal , Thrash Metal , 20 comments “Thrash has enjoyed a solid year thus far…Whether your poison has included the head-spinning technical fireworks of Cryptic Shift , the sprightly horns-up gallop of Kreator ’s inspired latest, lean, mean death-thrash of Nuclear Tomb , hard-hitting punch of Furi Helium , or rabidly nasty old school battering of Desolus , the legendary genre where many a metalhead cut their teeth has been on a solid streak. My issue with the modern thrash scene is the lack of genuinely exciting outfits releasing standout material, especially when stacked up to other preferred genres, such as the embarrassment of riches of the current death metal scene. Arriving with their full-length debut of retro-inspired modern thrash, UK outfit Imperium unleash debut album Exodus Unknown .” Thrash like no one’s watching. Illwind – The Unfolding at the End of Light Review By ClarkKent on July 22, 2026 in Reviews , Doom Metal , Epic Metal , Stoner Metal , 21 comments “Sometimes a new band starts up and chooses a godawful nameâlike Illwind . I’m not just referring to associations with flatulence eitherâthough that certainly doesn’t help. It’s just a difficult name to research, especially for a band with such a small web presence. Google will direct you to the Radiohead song, “Ill Wind,” or a 1960s psychedelic band from Boston called Ill Wind , among other surprisingly numerous use cases for the term “ill wind.” In fact, I began to have some doubts the band was real.” Frequent wind. Psycroptic – The Pulse of Annihilation Review By Owlswald on July 21, 2026 in Reviews , Death Metal , Technical Death Metal , 54 comments “ Psycroptic have been a cornerstone in the tech death scene since the turn of the century. Since then, these Tasmanian devils have churned out seven albums of inventive tech death, setting a standard that few have matched and even fewer have surpassed. 2015’s self-titled release marked the point at which Psycroptic truly settled into their modern form.” Psy-ops. Fuming Mouth – The Ringing Bell Review By Lavender Larcenist on July 21, 2026 in Reviews , Death Metal , Hardcore , 14 comments “An age-old challenge for critical houses like ours is that, while they simultaneously represent a cohesive vision, each editor should have their own unique voice. For me, it was one of my favorite albums that year and a record I spin frequently to this day.” Open wide! Carbon Tomb – Passage to a Neutron Star Review By Alekhines Gun on July 20, 2026 in Reviews , 65 comments “While any band, metal or not, would kill to have a classic in their discography, I often feel that having one can do their other albums a disservice. Some classics continue their perpetual status by virtue of self-fulfilling “this is good because we have been told it must be” prophecy ( Tomb of the Mutilated ), while some keep their status because fans refuse to accept anything else as even capable of touching this particular sound ( None So Vile , Dopethrone , Destroy the Opposition ) irrespective of the quality of follow-up releases. Ruyned – Profanum Sacrificium Review By Grin Reaper on July 20, 2026 in Reviews , Thrash Metal , 17 comments “Iâve been on a bit of a thrash bender in June, and Ruyned will conclude this monthâs kick for olâ Grin . The Romanian trio sends me deep into the heart of July with turbocharged, high-octane riffing and a moderate case of whiplash. Profanum Sacrificium , Ruyned âs sophomore LP, plays thrash the way the olde school intendedâat furious speeds with the subtlety of an uppercut to the jawbone.” Thrash in your face. Nunslaughter – Satanic Chaos Legions Review By Tyme on July 19, 2026 in Reviews , Death Metal , 24 comments “40 years, 34 past and present members, over 230 releases credited in the archives, and one migration from Pennsylvaniaâthis is the rap sheet for Cleveland, Ohio’s Nunslaughter , who have been jousting Jesus since 1987. Optimistic without being naïve, it’s a record that meets experimentation with open ears and a sense of wonder. Read More Ryuichi Sakamoto :: Beauty Posted on July 23, 2026 July 23, 2026 Guided by an adherence to a philosophy of “outernationalism”, Japanese composer Ryuichi Sakamoto’s overlooked 1989 record Beauty concluded a pivotal decade of transnational works and collaborations. An audio patchwork tapestry that defies “world music” in its modern conventions, Sakamoto corralled countless collaborators for the sessions (a few including Brian Wilson, Sly Dunbar, Robert Wyatt, Senegalese vocalist Youssou N’Dour, and Arto Lindsay). A self-proclaimed cultural map including Okinawan folk songs and a Rolling Stones cover, Beauty is a spectacle to behold, reaching beyond any regional boundaries. No Endtroducing: DJ Shadow’s Debut at 30 Posted on July 22, 2026 July 22, 2026 Filled with percussion pillaged from obscure funk cuts and interjections from early emcees like T La Rock and Kurtis Blow, Endtroducing rummages through the aboriginal dreamtime of hip hop just as it began its first period of slick commercial dominance. It also occasioned the creation of a new genre, trip hop, that quickly went on to mean something mostly different. But even though it used the past for parts and appeared at the close of the analog era, we’re still catching up to it. Who knows what sickness lurks in the hearts of bins? The Shadow knows. The Afterlives of Dub, 1995-2025 (A Mixtape) Posted on July 22, 2026 July 21, 2026 Once again, it’s dubwise summer. This is the season for tight rhythms, faded melodies and infinite echo. But rather than serve up another collection of classic Jamaican riddims, we decided to sketch out some of the afterlives of dub. Dub’s impact on experimental music at the turn of the twenty-first century is incalculable. And every sound that soothed the Y2K soul, from post-rock to trip-hop to illbient to dub techno, owes something to roots reggae’s downtempo electric stepchild. Over the past three decades, It has become one of the fundamental idioms of out music. This is our attempt to make our way through the haze. Transmissions :: Mouse on Mars on Lee “Scratch” Perry Posted on July 22, 2026 July 21, 2026 This week on Transmissions we’re joined by Jan St. Werner and Andi Toma, Mouse on Mars, calling in from Germany to discuss creating Spatial, No Problem with Lee “Scratch” Perry. The two have been friends since they were kids. In 1993, they formed Mouse on Mars, drawing from electronic traditions, live instrumentation, breakbeats, and a propensity for collaboration with other artists, including Mark E. Smith of The Fall, members of the National, and Stereolab—who, hey now, provide our show’s theme song: “Flashes in the Afternoon.” This week on Transmissions, Jan and Andi share memories of working on the album, with detours into discussion of some of their other collaborations, with artists like Mark E. Smith and another Transmissions favorite, Swamp Dogg. Styrofoam Winos :: The Aquarium Drunkard Interview Posted on July 21, 2026 July 21, 2026 Spend a few minutes talking with Styrofoam Winos and it becomes obvious why Any River feels so effortless. There are no fixed roles, no designated leader, and very little interest in conventional band hierarchy. Just three longtime friends following songs wherever they lead. We sat down with Joe Kenkel, Trevor Nikrant, and Lou Turner to talk collaboration, improvisation, everyday poetry, and the enduring rewards of making music together. Anatolian Psych Out: Volume Two Posted on July 21, 2026 July 21, 2026 A continuation rather than a sequel, Anatolian Psych Out: Volume Two ventures deeper into one of rock’s richest parallel histories. Seventeen selections from Turkey’s extraordinary late-’60s and ’70s flowering, where centuries-old musical traditions meet fuzz guitars, electric bağlama, and hypnotic grooves—music that remains as mysterious, transportive, and untethered by time as ever. Mark & Suzann Farmer :: Dreams Posted on July 20, 2026 July 17, 2026 You can practically taste the Chivas and smell the KOOL 100s smoldering in the ashtray. Fleetwood Mac’s “Dreams,” refracted through a homemade four-track in rural Washington. Released barely a year after Rumours , Mark & Suzann Farmer’s 1978 private-press version trades California studio polish for after-hours intimacy. Radio Free Aquarium Drunkard :: July 2026 Posted on July 19, 2026 July 19, 2026 Freeform transmissions from Radio Free Aquarium Drunkard on dublab. Airing every third Sunday of the month, RFAD on dublab features the pairing of Tyler Wilcox’s Doom and Gloom from the Tomb and Chad DePasquale’s New Happy Gathering. This month, Tyler kicks it off with a mix of cosmic summer jazz, new and old. Chad follows with an hour of garage rock, psychedelic soul, outsider folk & more. Sunday, 4-6pm PT. DIY Festivals, Coast to Coast :: Ghost Canyon, Fly Around Fest & Open Melody Posted on July 17, 2026 July 17, 2026 Beyond the festival-industrial complex, a different ecosystem is quietly thriving. We check in with the organizers behind three fiercely independent gatherings—Ghost Canyon, Fly Around Fest and Open Melody—to talk curation, community, risk, and why the best music festivals still feel like beautifully assembled mixtapes rather than content farms. Michael Brook :: The Aquarium Drunkard Interview Posted on July 16, 2026 July 16, 2026 Ambient guitar pioneer Michael Brook joins us to discuss his 1992 classic Cobalt Blue, which has recently been re-issued by label 4AD with new art and a dynamic remastering job. Brook joins us in the midst of preparing to present the Cobalt Blue material live for the first time in years to discuss the record, his time on the road with John Cale, and the durability of floppy disc technology. Horse Lords :: Demand to Be Taken to Heaven Alive! Posted on July 16, 2026 July 15, 2026 Horse Lords return with what may well be their strangest and prettiest album yet. Here the Baltimore-Berlin quartet adds Protestant hymn-singing, swamp boogie, early computer music and even robot funk to their signature brand of phase-shifting krautrock groove. It is an alluring, compelling mix that plays with themes of religious devotion and utopian longing. Fifteen years into their career, Horse Lords are still making some of the most radical, captivating music of our time. The Aquarium Drunkard Show: SIRIUS/XMU (7pm PDT, Channel 35) Posted on July 15, 2026 July 14, 2026 Broadcasting from northeast Los Angeles — the Aquarium Drunkard Show on SIRIUS/XMU, channel 35. 7pm California time, Wednesdays. Trees. Gardens. Fireplaces. Corridors. Birds overhead. Sun-faded. Handmade. 34.1090° N, 118.2334° W Aaron MF Olson :: Songs Album II Posted on July 15, 2026 July 14, 2026 Starkly naming a record Songs Album II is only an appropriate detour for a musician and composer like Aaron MF Olson. Sharing a sensibility with fellow “song” records by the likes of Jim O’Rourke or Van Dyke Parks, Olson’s conscious definition of such is a moving panorama: lavish, engaging and downright clever at the same time. With its flowering avant-pop arrangements, twisting ballads and warmly inviting hooks move effortlessly alongside pastoral landscapes where exotica meets Americana. But we also readily admit when we’re very late in discovering impressive talents, and we are definitely very late in discovering the wiles of Shabti from Portland, Maine. The lateness is even more inexcusable given the notable nature of Shabti’s lineup, which consists of drummer Ray Capizzo ( Falls Of Rauros , Obsidian Tongue , Panopticon -live), vocalist/guitarist Rob Cook ( Worm Altar ), and bassist Brendan Hayter ( Obsidian Tongue , Fires In The Distance ). When a band features talents such as those, you’d think smart people would have explored what they’ve been doing long before now. But alas, we didn’t — until now. Shabti now have their third album, Haze, Cacophony, And White Light , set for release on August 21st by the Contemn Light label. The album’s first single grabbed our attention like a big hand on the back of the neck, and we then grabbed back, seizing the opportunity to premiere a second single today. Continue reading » Posted by Islander at 9:12 am Tagged with: Shabti AN NCS PREMIERE: ENDSPACE — “FIRST TRANSMISSION” Premieres 1 Response » Jul 23 2026 (written by Islander) The copy/paste keyboard function is vital to preserving the sanity of people who write about metal in its global settings. Sometimes that’s because band names, album titles, or song names are in a language different from that of the writer. Sometimes it’s because metal bands have a tendency to just make up words for such things, and the words can be unpronounceable due to confounding juxtapositiions of consonants and vowels. And sometimes, but much more rarely, something significant is expressed in long strings of numbers. Which brings us to the Canadian band Endspace . The name Endspace can be typed without difficulty, but copy/pasting is necessary for the identification of its two members. They are: 040118180514 and 102119200914 . Equally strange, this isn’t the first time those two members have identified themselves numerically, even though Endspace is a new band — because they are also members of Fractal Generator , in which they are known by the same collections of digits. This might lead you to assume there are similarities between what Endspace is doing and what Fractal Generator have done. You wouldn’t be entirely wrong, but you wouldn’t be entirely right either. At a high level Fractal Generator lean into death metal, whereas Endspace lean into black metal. And yet they are only just leaning, because both bands put lots of often head-scrambling twists, turns, and layers into their musical (and lyrical) concoctions. In the case of Endspace , their first concoctions are encompassed by a debut album named First Contact that will be released on October 2nd by Transcending Obscurity Records . As the label says, it is “sci-fi themed black metal”, and it’s “light years ahead of what we’re accustomed to hearing”. Continue reading » Posted by Islander at 8:17 am Tagged with: Shtrigë AN NCS INTERVIEW: DAUÞUZ Interviews 1 Response » Jul 22 2026 (In this new interview our Comrade Aleks conversed with the two members of the Mining Black Metal band Dauþuz , whose new album Todeswerk: Uranium II was released in late May by Amor Fati Productions .) Having some knowledge of radionuclide technologies and the history of their application, I could not ignore the new album of the German black metal band Dauþuz entitled Todeswerk: Uranium II . The official press-release stated that the album’s concept was built around uranium mining in the Bohemian Joachimsthal after World War II and the inhuman conditions of workers who were forced to labour in the name of the Soviet Nuclear Program. But the story is more complicated as always, and it’s easy to find that for long years the town was almost the only source of uranium since WW I. Uranium mining was temporarily suspended due to increasing cancer deaths in 1929, yet despite attempts to improve workers’ conditions, the mortality rate continued to rise. It’s not a secret that things went really bad when Jáchymov was occupied by German troops during WW II, and fascists utilized Soviet prisoners in the name of the German Nuclear Program there. Needless to say, the situation changed vice-versa when the War was over, and everything was in ruins. As I reflected on this topic, I found some glimpses of the story of uranium mining in Africa… Did you know where the uranium used to make the bombs that destroyed Hiroshima and Nagasaki came from? Go and check, there was no fun there either. So, okay, uranium mining in Africa, Canada, and the USA – back then no one gave a damn about the environment or miners’ lives either of course. There was no any trace of humanism in this field for decades, and I bet there is nothing like this in some Third World countries nowadays as well. Todeswerk: Uranium II tells the story of suffering, forced labour, and death in the name of progress and power in a most artistic way – both straightforward and epic. The interview with Dauþuz ’ members Aragonyth and Syderyth was tough sometimes due to the difficult topics we discussed, but we managed to keep it diplomatic to some degree. That’s not to say that they throw things against the wall to see what sticks, or drip ingredients into test tubes to see whether the fluids change colors or explode. They are instead quite obviously very demanding of themselves and very meticulous in their planning and execution, it’s just that they march to the beat of their own drummer; their musical interests are wide-ranging and they’ve never been satisfied by remaining in the same musical space for very long. Because of that it’s probably not hugely helpful in our introduction of their new album to provide a history of the band’s releases (and the changing musical forays they have represented) across their 26 years of activity. It’s maybe more helpful to just focus on the new music and leave the past behind. And yes, nine years after their last album, Viscera/// have a new full-length titled 4. There’s a commonly quoted, but often misinterpreted, maxim which states that as you grow older there comes a time when you must “put away childish things.” What it actually means is that the process of becoming an adult means growing beyond selfishness and short-sighted, self-serving impulsivity to consider the people and the world around you… but it’s often taken as a claim that one should give up the things you enjoy, your passions, your hobbies, your interests, in order to display some sort of boring, performative “maturity”. It’s a sentiment that afflicts a lot of bands too, especially when they suddenly decide to throw in an unnecessary ballad or some generically pretentious prog-parts as a signal to their audience that they’ve finally “grown up”. Luckily for all of us, however, as much as Canadian genre-splicing jesters Protest the Hero have grown and developed over the years I’m happy to report that they still possess that same shamelessly nerdy, Theatre-Kid Pop-Punk meets Dream Theatre -loving, DnD-playing, Power-Prog energy now as they did way back before they could even grow some proper beards. But the song we’re about to premiere (and the whole album it comes from) has turned out to be a very happy surprise for this writer. It’s a surprise because I wasn’t previously familiar with the band, and because their style of music is outside the kind of gnashing, gnarly, macabre, and malignant brands of extremity to which I usually subject my crippled ears. And it’s a spice-of-life surprise, because it turns out to be some of the most completely captivating music I’ve heard all year. The band is Argul , a quartet from Lima, Peru, consisting of guitarist/vocalist Mauricio Guerrero , guitarist/vocalist Jose María Venturi , bassist Jian Holmes , and drummer Alonso Guerrero . They independently released a four-song demo in 2023, and now their debut album Soledad & Orgullo is set for release on August 21st by Personal Records . That album is the home of the remarkable song we’re now premiering, an enthralling track named “ Kamog “.
